answer:
Definition

Idempotency means performing the same operation multiple times produces the same result as performing it once. In APIs, a request is idempotent if sending it 1 time or 100 times has the same effect on the server. GET, PUT, and DELETE are naturally idempotent; POST is not.

Concrete Use-Case

In Manoj's HR Leave Management System, the leave approval endpoint (PATCH /api/leaves/:id/approve) is idempotent — approving an already-approved leave returns success without changing anything. This matters because the manager's browser might retry on a network timeout, and the system must not send duplicate approval notifications or trigger the workflow twice.

Visual Guide

Code Example

// Idempotent leave approval — safe to retry
fastify.patch('/api/leaves/:id/approve', async (req, reply) => {
  const leave = await prisma.leave.findUnique({ where: { id: req.params.id } });

  if (!leave) return reply.code(404).send({ error: 'Leave not found' });

  // Already approved? Return success without side effects
  if (leave.status === 'approved') {
    return { message: 'Already approved', leave }; // Idempotent!
  }

  const updated = await prisma.leave.update({
    where: { id: req.params.id },
    data: { status: 'approved', approvedBy: req.user.id, approvedAt: new Date() }
  });

  // Side effect only on actual state change
  await notifyEmployee(updated.employeeId, 'Your leave was approved');
  return { message: 'Approved', leave: updated };
});

// Making POST idempotent with idempotency keys
fastify.post('/api/leaves', async (req, reply) => {
  const idempotencyKey = req.headers['x-idempotency-key'];

  if (idempotencyKey) {
    const existing = await prisma.leave.findFirst({
      where: { idempotencyKey }
    });
    if (existing) return reply.code(200).send(existing); // Return cached result
  }

  const leave = await prisma.leave.create({
    data: { ...req.body, idempotencyKey }
  });
  reply.code(201).send(leave);
});

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: Assuming DELETE is always idempotent. DELETE /api/leaves/:id is idempotent (deleting twice = same result), but DELETE /api/leaves?status=pending is NOT — new pending leaves created between calls get deleted too.

Mitigation: Make DELETE operate on specific resource IDs, not query filters. For bulk operations, use a POST with an explicit list of IDs.

Definition

Idempotency solves the retry safety problem in distributed systems. Networks are unreliable — requests can timeout, be duplicated by load balancers, or retried by clients. Without idempotency, a retried payment request could charge the customer twice.

Concrete Use-Case

In Manoj's ContactsHub, the bulk CSV upload endpoint (POST /api/contacts/bulk) processes 500 contacts per batch. If the upload times out and the user clicks "Upload" again, without idempotency they'd get duplicate contacts. The solution: hash the CSV content as an idempotency key. If the same file is uploaded again, return the previous result instead of re-importing.

Problems Solved

  • Payment safety: Prevent double-charging when a payment request is retried
  • Data integrity: No duplicate records from retried POST requests
  • User experience: Users can safely click "Submit" multiple times without consequences
  • Webhook reliability: Webhook receivers can safely re-process the same event

Visual Guide

Code Example

// Idempotency middleware for Fastify
const idempotencyStore = new Map(); // Use Redis in production

fastify.addHook('preHandler', async (req, reply) => {
  if (req.method !== 'POST') return; // Only POST needs idempotency keys

  const key = req.headers['x-idempotency-key'];
  if (!key) return; // No key provided, process normally

  const cached = idempotencyStore.get(key);
  if (cached) {
    reply.code(cached.statusCode).send(cached.body);
    return reply; // Short-circuit — don't process again
  }
});

fastify.addHook('onSend', async (req, reply, payload) => {
  const key = req.headers['x-idempotency-key'];
  if (key && req.method === 'POST') {
    idempotencyStore.set(key, {
      statusCode: reply.statusCode,
      body: payload
    });
    // Set TTL: clean up after 24 hours
    setTimeout(() => idempotencyStore.delete(key), 86400000);
  }
});

// Client usage
const response = await fetch('/api/contacts/bulk', {
  method: 'POST',
  headers: {
    'Content-Type': 'application/json',
    'X-Idempotency-Key': crypto.randomUUID() // unique per user action
  },
  body: JSON.stringify({ contacts: csvData })
});

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: Using timestamps or sequential IDs as idempotency keys. They're not unique enough and can collide across users or browser tabs.

Mitigation: Use crypto.randomUUID() generated on the client when the user initiates the action (not on each retry). Store the key in state so retries reuse the same key.

Definition

Consistency models solve the problem of defining what data a reader sees after a writer updates it, especially across distributed nodes. In production, choosing the wrong consistency model means users see stale data, phantom reads, or lost updates.

Concrete Use-Case

In Manoj's HR Leave System, the leave balance requires strong consistency — when an employee's leave is approved, their balance must immediately reflect the deduction. If another manager queries the balance 1ms later, they must see the updated value. Meanwhile, the CRM Dashboard uses eventual consistency for the sales leaderboard — if a rep closes a deal, it's acceptable for the leaderboard to update within 5-10 seconds.

Problems Solved

  • Data correctness: Prevent overselling, double-booking, or incorrect balances
  • Performance trade-offs: Eventual consistency allows caching and replication lag, improving read speed
  • System design: Guides decisions about read replicas, caching layers, and transaction boundaries
  • User expectations: Match technical consistency with what users expect to see

Visual Guide

Code Example

// Strong consistency: PostgreSQL transaction for leave balance
async function deductLeave(employeeId, days) {
  return prisma.$transaction(async (tx) => {
    const emp = await tx.employee.findUnique({
      where: { id: employeeId }
    });
    if (emp.leaveBalance < days) throw new Error('Insufficient balance');
    return tx.employee.update({
      where: { id: employeeId },
      data: { leaveBalance: { decrement: days } }
    });
  }, { isolationLevel: 'Serializable' }); // Strongest guarantee
}

// Eventual consistency: Cached leaderboard
async function getSalesLeaderboard() {
  const cached = await redis.get('leaderboard');
  if (cached) return JSON.parse(cached); // might be 10s stale

  const fresh = await db.collection('sales').aggregate([
    { $group: { _id: '$repId', total: { $sum: '$amount' } } },
    { $sort: { total: -1 } },
    { $limit: 10 }
  ]).toArray();

  await redis.setEx('leaderboard', 10, JSON.stringify(fresh));
  return fresh;
}

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: Using read replicas for writes-followed-by-reads. The user updates their profile, the page refreshes, but reads from a replica that hasn't synced yet — showing old data.

Mitigation: Implement "read-your-writes" consistency: after a write, read from the primary for that user's session (e.g., sticky sessions or a short cache bypass window).

Top 3 Consistency Model Mistakes

Mistake 1: Using Eventual Consistency for Financial Data

Caching account balances or inventory counts with stale reads, allowing overselling or double-spending.

// ❌ BAD: Cached balance allows overdraft
async function checkBalance(userId) {
  const cached = await redis.get(`balance:${userId}`);
  if (cached) return parseInt(cached); // could be stale!
  const user = await db.user.findUnique({ where: { id: userId } });
  await redis.setEx(`balance:${userId}`, 60, user.balance);
  return user.balance;
}

// ✅ GOOD: Read from primary for balance checks
async function checkBalance(userId) {
  const user = await prisma.employee.findUnique({
    where: { id: userId },
    select: { leaveBalance: true }
  });
  return user.leaveBalance; // always current
}

Mistake 2: Not Handling Read-After-Write Inconsistency

User updates their data, page refreshes, but the GET request hits a read replica that hasn't synced.

// ❌ BAD: User sees old data after saving
await fetch('/api/profile', { method: 'PUT', body: JSON.stringify(newData) });
const profile = await fetch('/api/profile'); // might hit stale replica

// ✅ GOOD: Use optimistic UI update
await fetch('/api/profile', { method: 'PUT', body: JSON.stringify(newData) });
setProfile(newData); // Update UI immediately with the data we just sent
// Background: eventual sync will catch up

Mistake 3: Ignoring Consistency in Caching Layers

Caching data without proper invalidation, serving stale data indefinitely.

// ❌ BAD: Cache never invalidated
async function getEmployee(id) {
  const cached = cache.get(`emp:${id}`);
  if (cached) return cached; // stale forever if never invalidated
  const emp = await prisma.employee.findUnique({ where: { id } });
  cache.set(`emp:${id}`, emp);
  return emp;
}

// ✅ GOOD: Invalidate on write + TTL fallback
async function updateEmployee(id, data) {
  const updated = await prisma.employee.update({ where: { id }, data });
  cache.delete(`emp:${id}`); // Immediate invalidation
  return updated;
}

async function getEmployee(id) {
  const cached = cache.get(`emp:${id}`);
  if (cached) return cached;
  const emp = await prisma.employee.findUnique({ where: { id } });
  cache.set(`emp:${id}`, emp, { ttl: 300_000 }); // 5min TTL as safety net
  return emp;
}

Concrete Use-Case

In Manoj's HR Leave System, the Socket.io notification for leave approval needed causal consistency: the notification must only arrive AFTER the database write completes. Initially, the notification was sent in parallel with the DB update, causing the employee to see "Leave approved" but the leave list still showed "pending". Moving the notification after await prisma.leave.update() fixed the ordering.

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: Assuming all reads in a microservices architecture are strongly consistent. Each service might cache data independently, creating inconsistencies across the system.

Mitigation: Use event-driven architecture (pub/sub) to propagate changes. When the Leave Service updates a balance, it publishes an event that the Notification Service and Dashboard Service consume to update their local state.

Top 3 Concurrency Control Mistakes

Mistake 1: Race Conditions in Shared State

Two requests read-then-write the same resource without locking, causing lost updates.

// ❌ BAD: Race condition — two managers approve simultaneously
app.patch('/api/leaves/:id/approve', async (req, res) => {
  const leave = await prisma.leave.findUnique({ where: { id: req.params.id } });
  if (leave.status !== 'pending') return res.status(400).send('Already processed');

  // ⚠️ Between findUnique and update, another request can also pass the check!
  await prisma.leave.update({
    where: { id: req.params.id },
    data: { status: 'approved' }
  });
});

// ✅ GOOD: Atomic conditional update
app.patch('/api/leaves/:id/approve', async (req, res) => {
  try {
    const updated = await prisma.leave.update({
      where: { id: req.params.id, status: 'pending' }, // atomic check
      data: { status: 'approved', approvedBy: req.user.id }
    });
    res.json(updated);
  } catch (e) {
    if (e.code === 'P2025') res.status(409).send('Already processed');
  }
});

Mistake 2: Not Using Database Transactions for Multi-Step Operations

Updating related records without a transaction, risking partial updates on failure.

// ❌ BAD: If step 2 fails, step 1 already committed
await prisma.leave.update({ where: { id }, data: { status: 'approved' } });
await prisma.employee.update({ // might fail!
  where: { id: leave.employeeId },
  data: { leaveBalance: { decrement: leave.days } }
});

// ✅ GOOD: All-or-nothing transaction
await prisma.$transaction([
  prisma.leave.update({ where: { id }, data: { status: 'approved' } }),
  prisma.employee.update({
    where: { id: leave.employeeId },
    data: { leaveBalance: { decrement: leave.days } }
  })
]);

Mistake 3: Deadlocks from Inconsistent Lock Ordering

Two transactions lock resources in different order, each waiting for the other.

// ❌ BAD: Transaction A locks employee then leave; Transaction B locks leave then employee
// → Deadlock!

// ✅ GOOD: Always lock in the same order (alphabetical: employee → leave)
await prisma.$transaction(async (tx) => {
  await tx.employee.update({ where: { id: empId }, data: { ... } }); // always first
  await tx.leave.update({ where: { id: leaveId }, data: { ... } });   // always second
});

Concrete Use-Case

In Manoj's HR Leave System, the 3-level approval chain (team lead → manager → HR) needed concurrency control. Without it, two approvers at the same level could both approve, skipping the chain. An optimistic lock using a version column ensured only one approval per level succeeds.

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: Over-locking with pessimistic locks (SELECT FOR UPDATE) causing performance bottlenecks under high traffic.

Mitigation: Use optimistic locking (version column + conditional update) for read-heavy workloads. Reserve pessimistic locking for critical financial operations where conflicts are frequent.

Definition

Concurrency Control manages simultaneous access to shared resources (database rows, files, in-memory state) to prevent data corruption. In web apps, it ensures that when two users update the same record at the same time, the system handles it correctly — either by locking, versioning, or queuing.

Concrete Use-Case

In Manoj's HR Leave System, when an employee submits a leave request, the system must atomically check the leave balance and decrement it. Without concurrency control, two simultaneous requests for the last 5 days of leave could both succeed, resulting in a -5 balance. Prisma's $transaction with serializable isolation prevents this.

Visual Guide

Code Example

// Optimistic locking with version column
async function updateLeave(id, data, expectedVersion) {
  try {
    return await prisma.leave.update({
      where: {
        id,
        version: expectedVersion // only succeeds if version matches
      },
      data: {
        ...data,
        version: { increment: 1 } // bump version
      }
    });
  } catch (e) {
    if (e.code === 'P2025') {
      throw new Error('Conflict: record was modified by another user. Please refresh.');
    }
    throw e;
  }
}

// Pessimistic locking with SELECT FOR UPDATE
async function deductBalance(employeeId, days) {
  return prisma.$transaction(async (tx) => {
    // Lock the row — other transactions wait
    const [emp] = await tx.$queryRaw`
      SELECT * FROM employees WHERE id = ${employeeId} FOR UPDATE
    `;

    if (emp.leave_balance < days) {
      throw new Error('Insufficient balance');
    }

    return tx.employee.update({
      where: { id: employeeId },
      data: { leaveBalance: { decrement: days } }
    });
  });
}

Debugging Narrative

In the HR Leave System, Manoj discovered that two concurrent leave requests for the same employee both succeeded despite insufficient balance. The root cause: the check-then-update was non-atomic. The fix was wrapping both operations in a serializable transaction, ensuring the balance check and decrement happen atomically.

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: Using application-level locks (in-memory mutexes) in a multi-instance deployment. Each server has its own lock, so they don't protect against cross-instance concurrency.

Mitigation: Use database-level locks (transactions, FOR UPDATE) or distributed locks (Redis SETNX with TTL). Application-level locks only work for single-instance deployments.
